title: Domain Adaptation
concept: Domain adaptation is the process of adjusting a model so it generalizes from a labeled source domain to an unlabeled target domain.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main purpose of ImageNet-C?

options.text: To benchmark how robust classification models are against diverse image corruptions and noise
To speed up the training of vision transformers
To provide high-quality pixel coordinates for object detection

exercises.explain: ImageNet-C applies corruptions (blur, noise) to test a model's robustness and degradation profile.

cards.title: Bridging the gap
cards.body: Imagine you have 1 million labeled synthetic drawings of cars, but no budget to label real-world photos. You want your model to work on real photos. How do you transfer the learning without starting your labeling from scratch? You adapt the domains.

cards.title: Domain Adaptation
cards.body: Domain Adaptation is a subfield of transfer learning. It aims to align the feature representations of a labeled **source domain** and an unlabeled (or sparsely labeled) **target domain** so the classifier works on both.

cards.title: A chef in a new kitchen
cards.body: Imagine a professional chef who moves to a new country. The ingredients and stoves (target domain) are slightly different, but the core cooking principles (source domain) are the same. Instead of relearning how to cook, the chef spends a few days adapting to the local tools.

cards.title: Adversarial Alignment
cards.body: Engineers use a **Domain Discriminator** network. During training, the main model extracts features, while the discriminator tries to guess if the feature came from a synthetic drawing or a real photo. By training the model to fool the discriminator, it learns features that are domain-invariant.

cards.title: You don't always need target labels
cards.body: You might think adapting to a new domain requires hiring human annotators to label images. In reality, **Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA)** techniques align domains using only *unlabeled* target images, saving massive labeling costs.

cards.title: Feature Alignment
cards.body: How domain adaptation aligns internal representations:

left.text:
Model groups drawings and photos separately.
Classifier trained on drawings fails on photos.
High domain discrepancy.

right.text:
Features map to same space regardless of style.
Classifier works on both drawings and photos.
Low domain discrepancy.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA)?
exercises.explain: UDA aligns feature spaces between a labeled source and unlabeled target to avoid the cost of new labels.

options.text: To align model features using labeled source data and unlabeled target data so the model generalizes to the target domain
To remove all layers from a convolutional network
To convert bounding boxes into natural text captions
To double the size of the training dataset automatically

exercises.prompt: True or False: A Domain Discriminator is trained to help the model learn features that distinguish source and target styles as clearly as possible.
exercises.explain: False. While the discriminator tries to tell them apart, the model is trained to *fool* it, resulting in domain-invariant features.
Domain-invariant features represent the abstract concept (e.g., shape) rather than domain style (e.g., drawing vs photo).
exercises.sentence: Features that look identical regardless of whether they come from the source or target domain are called domain-___.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a common domain adaptation technique?
exercises.explain: DANN uses adversarial training to force feature extractors to build domain-invariant representations.

exercises.prompt: You have a massive dataset of labeled driving video from California (source). You want to deploy the car in snow-covered streets in Norway (target). How do you apply unsupervised domain adaptation?

options.text: Train your model on California videos, and use unlabeled Norway driving videos in an adversarial loop to force the model's feature extractor to learn representations that align sunny and snowy roads.
Discard California data and hire people in Norway to manually label millions of new frames.
Deploy the model directly in Norway without changes, assuming snow has no effect on pixels.
Train only on the Norway dataset without using labels.

exercises.explain: Adversarial feature alignment lets you leverage the labeling work from California while adapting the model's eyes to Norwegian weather using cheap, unlabeled videos.
